title: When to Start Online Tutoring: A Parent's Timing Guide
lead: Is your child ready for online tutoring? The answer depends less on age and more on a few practical signs. Here's how to decide.

sections.title: The Early Years (K-2)
content: Younger kids can do online tutoring, but sessions need to be shorter—15 to 20 minutes max. They also need a parent nearby to help with tech and keep them focused. If your child can sit for a short video call with grandparents, they can likely handle a brief tutoring session.

sections.title: Middle Elementary (3-5)
content: This is when many kids start to benefit. They can usually follow instructions, use a mouse or trackpad, and stay engaged for 30–45 minutes. Reading and math foundations are being set, so early help can prevent gaps from widening.

sections.title: Signs Your Child Might Need Help Now
content: Frustration with homework, slipping grades, or "I don't get it" more often than usual. Some kids just need a few sessions to get back on track. Others need ongoing support. Starting earlier usually means less catch-up later.

sections.title: When to Wait
content: If your child is resistant to screens, struggles with sitting still, or needs hands-on materials, in-person might work better for now. You can revisit online tutoring as they get older.

cta.title: See If Online Tutoring Fits
cta.text: Book a free session with GuruForU to see if your child is ready.

faq.question: What age is best to start online tutoring?
faq.answer: It depends less on age and more on readiness. Middle elementary (grades 3–5) is when many kids benefit most—they can follow instructions and stay engaged for 30–45 minutes.

faq.question: Can younger kids (K-2) do online tutoring?
faq.answer: Yes, but sessions should be shorter (15–20 minutes) and a parent should be nearby for tech help and focus. If your child can do a short video call with grandparents, they can likely handle it.

faq.question: When should I wait before starting online tutoring?
faq.answer: If your child resists screens, struggles to sit still, or needs hands-on materials, in-person might work better for now. You can revisit online tutoring as they get older.

faq.question: What are signs my child needs help now?
faq.answer: Frustration with homework, slipping grades, or "I don't get it" more often than usual. Starting earlier usually means less catch-up later.
